one_line_takeaway: Hidden, monetized LLM-agent skill packages (SKILL.md) can be cheaply extracted verbatim or semantically via ordinary black-box prompting across five commercial models and multiple agent platforms, and layered heuristic defenses reduce but never eliminate the leakage, especially under translation/rewrite transformations.
key_finding: Black-box skill extraction is easy, cheap, and broadly effective, and even the naive baseline is a strong attack. With no scenario and no structure (NO,NO), the average across five models already reaches 48% EM, 79.95% ROUGE-L, 95.11% cosine similarity, and 0.91 LLM-leakage ratio. Few-shot prompting best preserves lexical/semantic fidelity (NO,FS raises average ROUGE-L to 90.74% and cosine to 97.34%), while Chain-of-Thought best elicits exact disclosure (NO,COT gives the highest average EM at 72% and highest LLM-leakage ratio at 0.95). Vulnerability is model-heterogeneous: GPT-5 and Kimi-k2.5 are the most exposed, MiniMax-M2.7 highly exposed but prompt-sensitive, DeepSeek-V3.2 shows low EM but high semantic leakage, and Claude-haiku-4.5 is the most strategy-sensitive (most resistant). The attack transfers to commercial coding agents (Claude Code 10-60% EM; Codex near-zero EM but high semantic leakage, attributed to output encoding artifacts not protection) and to frontier web platforms (ChatGPT/GPT-5.4 and Claude/Sonnet-4.6). On defense: the input detector scored perfectly on the authors' balanced set (TPR 1.00, FPR 0.00, F1 1.00); SkillGuard-5 drives EM to 0% everywhere but leaves semantic residue; the output-phase LAN filter drops EM to 0% in every row and, under (NO,NO), cuts ROUGE-L/cosine/LLM from 80%/95%/0.91 to 2%/14%/0.05 with 0.00 FPR. Crucially, transformation attacks defeat this: translation/rewrite push EM to 0% in 11/12 settings yet keep the LLM-leakage ratio at 0.85-0.96 (rewrite retains ROUGE-L 58-79%, cosine 90-96%), and cosine filtering becomes unstable under rewriting. The overarching claim: defenses reduce but cannot remove leakage, and a single success is enough.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first systematic study of 'skill stealing': a black-box extraction attack in which an ordinary user of an LLM-agent service recovers the hidden, proprietary SKILL.md capability packages the agent loads at runtime. Unlike ordinary system-prompt leakage, skills bundle instructions, curated workflows, templates, scripts, and domain knowledge, so leakage is directly actionable for copying, redistribution, and resale, framing this as a copyright/economic threat, not just a confidentiality one. The authors' own contributions are: (1) formulating and taxonomizing skill stealing by adapting prior prompt-stealing attack families into a 12-cell strategy space (4 scenario x 3 structure); (2) an automated skill-stealing-prompt generation agent (seed generation -> scenario+structure optimization -> embedding-based diversity filtering) that yields a reproducible 120-prompt benchmark; (3) an empirical evaluation across the OpenCode agent framework, five commercial LLMs, two coding agents (Claude Code, Codex), and two web platforms (ChatGPT, Claude web); and (4) a three-stage defense suite (input detector, inference-phase SkillGuard-5 prompt, output-phase LAN filter) that they show reduces but does not eliminate leakage.
threat_model: A realistic black-box adversary who is an ordinary user interacting with a deployed agent only through its public UI/API. The adversary can issue arbitrary multi-round natural-language queries and adapt based on responses, but has NO access to model weights, system configs, local SKILL.md files, or orchestration logic. Four-stage leakage path (Fig. 3): (1) attacker sends a crafted adversarial prompt via API; (2) the agent reads the relevant local SKILL.md during execution; (3) the agent reproduces that hidden content in its response, verbatim or in a semantics-preserving reformulation (paraphrase/translation/summary); (4) attacker collects the returned text, recovering the skill without touching the backend. Attacker goal: recover the full SKILL.md (metadata, description, workflow instructions, scripts, config) either verbatim or with high semantic similarity; the workflow instructions are treated as the most valuable target. Defender is the service provider with full control of the deployed system (can modify system instructions, inject defense prompts, alter skill injection, and add input/output inspection). A single successful extraction suffices to cause copyright harm, so the attack being cheap and repeatable is central to the model.
limitations: Authors acknowledge the study centers on one representative primary target skill (find-skills) and a fixed set of commercial systems; broader skills appear only in an ablation (Fig. 6). LAN's residual weakness is that its thresholds (tau_llm=0.60, tau_nv=0.80) remain too permissive for heavily transformed leakage - the hardest case (HY,COT) still leaves filtered ROUGE-L/cosine/LLM at 17%/71%/0.39. Beyond the stated limits: the input detector's perfect TPR/FPR is measured only against benign/attack prompts the authors themselves generated with GPT-5.4, so it may overstate real-world robustness against unseen attack phrasings; the leakage metrics lean heavily on GPT-5.4 as judge (also the seed/negative generator), a potential circularity; the whole result rests on a single agent framework (OpenCode) for the main experiment; and there is no human evaluation of whether 'high semantic similarity' truly equals a commercially usable clone. No formal or provable defense is offered - all defenses are heuristic and shown to be bypassable.

datasets_used: Custom skill-stealing benchmark of 120 prompts (10 GPT-5.4 seed prompts x 12 scenario-structure strategy combinations)

10 most-popular skills installed from skills.sh (find-skills [primary target], vercel-react-best-practices, frontend-design, web-design-guidelines, remotion-best-practices, microsoft-foundry, azure-ai, azure-deploy, azure-diagnostics, azure-prepare)
Balanced 240-prompt input-detector set (120 attack prompts on find-skills + 120 benign find-skills-related prompts generated by GPT-5.4)
Transformation-attack set (translation-to-Chinese and rewrite variants) over the 12 strategy combinations for output-defense stress testing
quantitative_results: Baseline attack (NO scenario, NO structure), avg over 5 models: 48% EM, 79.95% ROUGE-L, 95.11% cosine similarity, 0.91 LLM-leakage ratio
Best few-shot attack (NO,FS): avg ROUGE-L 90.74%, cosine 97.34%
Best CoT attack (NO,COT): highest avg EM 72%, highest LLM-leakage ratio 0.95
Input-phase detector: TPR 1.00, FPR 0.00, F1 1.00 on the 240-prompt balanced set

Inference defenses at (NO,NO) baseline: instruction defense leaves 18% EM / 57.65% ROUGE-L / 88.97% cosine / 0.76 LLM; sandwich defense leaves 22% EM / 64.46% ROUGE-L / 89.86% cosine / 0.75 LLM; SkillGuard-5 reduces EM to 0% across all 12 cells but leaves semantic residue

Output-phase LAN filter: EM reduced to 0% in every setting, FPR 0.00; under (NO,NO) ROUGE-L/cosine/LLM fall from 80%/95%/0.91 to 2%/14%/0.05; hardest case (HY,COT) filtered residual 17% ROUGE-L / 71% cosine / 0.39 LLM (vs 58%/88%/0.81 unfiltered)
Transformation attacks evade filtering: translation gives EM 0% in 11/12 settings yet LLM-leakage 0.91-0.96; rewrite gives EM 0% in 11/12 yet ROUGE-L 58.17-79.24%, cosine 89.72-95.67%, LLM 0.85-0.95
Cross-platform: Claude Code (backed by MiniMax-M2.7) EM in 10-60% range with high ROUGE-L/cosine; Codex (backed by GPT-5) near-zero EM (encoding artifacts) but high ROUGE-L/cosine/LLM leakage
Diversity filter threshold tau=0.75 (text-embedding-3-small); LAN dual thresholds tau_llm=0.60, tau_nv=0.80

Reported skill-economy scale: skills.sh dashboard 90,368 published skills, 9,485 publishers, 24.3M cumulative installs (as of 2026-03-31); Claw Mart marketplace 2,000+ listings, $100,000+ earned by creators, example skill priced at $99
baselines_compared: Attack baseline: direct extraction with no scenario and no structure (NO,NO), shown to already be a strong attack
Prior prompt-stealing attack families adapted as strategies: scenario rationalization (Educational, Hypothetical, Roleplay) and structure injection (Chain-of-Thought, Few-Shot Demonstration)
Inference-phase defense baselines from prior work: instruction defense and sandwich defense, compared against the proposed SkillGuard-5
Output-phase filter baselines: exact-match (EM) filtering and cosine-similarity filtering, compared against the proposed LAN (LLM And NVRecall) filter
Target models compared for vulnerability: GPT-5, MiniMax-M2.7, Kimi-k2.5, DeepSeek-V3.2, Claude-haiku-4.5 (plus GPT-5.4 and Sonnet-4.6 on web platforms)
